entries.name: Glucocorticoids (class — mechanism, systemic effects, ADRs, interactions)
entries.memo: They switch off inflammation by blocking **NF-κB**: no prostaglandins or leukotrienes. They treat almost everything, but the bill is **iatrogenic Cushing's**.

segments.label: Mechanism of Action
segments.value: lipophilic (steroid structure, like cholesterol). They bind **intracellular receptors**: **GR** (glucocorticoid receptor, activated at **high** concentrations) and **MR** (mineralocorticoid, **high affinity**, activated at low conc). The receptor is complexed with **HSP** in the cytosol → on binding it is released, **dimerizes**, enters the nucleus, binds the **GRE** (glucocorticoid responsive element) → modulates **gene transcription**. **Anti-inflammatory action:** inhibition of **NF-κB** (↑ inhibitors, ↓ expression; genomic and non-genomic mechanism) → ↓ COX-2, iNOS, cytokines; **↑ annexin-1** → inhibition of **phospholipase A2** → ↓ arachidonic acid → ↓ **prostaglandins AND leukotrienes**.

segments.label: Molecular Target
segments.value: GR and MR receptors (intracellular transcription factors).

segments.label: Pharmacokinetic Parameters (class)
segments.value: excellent absorption by **all routes** (systemic even from intra-articular/topical/inhaled). Cortisol → **transcortin** + albumin; the synthetic ones bind **mainly albumin**. **Extensive hepatic first-pass metabolism** (caution in patients with liver disease); glucuronide/sulfate conjugation → renal elimination. ⚠️ **Peak not immediate** → **not suitable for acute emergencies**. Classification by potency/duration (see individual agents).

segments.label: Clinical Indications
segments.value: **replacement therapy** (adrenal insufficiency → hydrocortisone); **abnormal immune response** (immunosuppression in transplantation; autoimmune: asthma, rheumatoid arthritis, lupus, COPD; hypersensitivity/allergies); **oncology** (leukaemias, lymphomas — selective action on T lymphocytes, + chemotherapeutics); **oedema** (cerebral, trauma). Effects on formed elements: ↓ eosinophils/T lymphocytes/macrophages/dendritic cells/mast cells; **↑ circulating PMNs** (demargination + blockade of tissue migration); little effect on B lymphocytes (cellular immunity > humoral).

segments.label: Common Adverse Reactions (ADR)
segments.value: they reflect the physiological systemic effects. Metabolism: **hyperglycaemia** (↑ gluconeogenesis/glucose-6-phosphatase; insulin resistance), protein catabolism → **steroid myopathy**, fat redistribution → **moon/cushingoid facies**. Salt-and-water (MR): **hypertension, oedema**; ↑ Ca excretion → **osteoporosis**. CV: ↑ sensitivity to catecholamines (↑ β1/α1) + RAAS. Gastric: ↓ prostaglandins → **peptic ulcer**. CNS: behavioural/mood disturbances, alteration of the sleep-wake rhythm. Endocrine alterations; **↓ GH in children**; cataract/vision disturbances; **immunosuppression → infections**. ⚠️ **From abrupt withdrawal** → **acute adrenal insufficiency** (suppressed axis) → **gradual tapered withdrawal** (prefer short half-life and/or alternate days).

segments.label: Resistance / Efficacy Limits
segments.value: **rapid tolerance** to the effects; not suitable for emergencies; **contraindications** (chronic use): hypertension/heart disease, **diabetes**, **ulcer**, psychosis/schizophrenia, **osteoporosis**, **glaucoma**, **infections**.

segments.label: Drug Combinations / Adjuvants
segments.value: **pharmacodynamic interactions** (antagonism with **antihypertensives**; they alter antidiabetics/hypoglycaemics/lipid-lowering drugs; **+ NSAIDs/ASA → ↑ ulcer risk** and pressor effects) and **pharmacokinetic** ones (**induction of hepatic cytochromes**).

entries.name: Hydrocortisone (= cortisol) / Cortisone
entries.memo: **Identical to cortisol** (1:1 gluco/mineralocorticoid): it is the **replacement therapy** for Addison's, not the anti-inflammatory of choice.

segments.label: Mechanism of Action
segments.value: reference glucocorticoid (endogenous); **glucocorticoid : mineralocorticoid activity = 1:1**.

segments.label: Molecular Target
segments.value: GR + MR.

segments.label: Pharmacokinetic Parameters
segments.value: **short duration of action (8-12 h)**; reference equivalent dose ~**20 mg**. **Succinate** ester (hydrophilic) → IV in emergencies; **acetate/butyrate** esters (lipophilic) → cutaneous preparations. **Cortisone** = prodrug, converted in vivo into cortisol.

segments.label: Clinical Indications
segments.value: **replacement therapy** for adrenal insufficiency (acute and chronic) — of choice because it maintains **both** activities (GC + MC); emergencies (shock, anaphylaxis) by the IV route (succinate ester).

segments.label: Common Adverse Reactions (ADR)
segments.value: as for the class.

segments.label: Resistance / Efficacy Limits
segments.value: as for the class.

segments.label: Drug Combinations / Adjuvants
segments.value: as for the class.

entries.name: Prednisone / Prednisolone
entries.memo: **Prodrug**: the liver activates it to prednisoLone. In patients with liver disease the conversion fails → **prednisoLone** is given directly.

segments.label: Mechanism of Action
segments.value: cortisol analogues with a **double bond at 1-2** → ↑ anti-inflammatory glucocorticoid activity, ↓ mineralocorticoid (MC ~0.3-0.5).

segments.label: Molecular Target
segments.value: GR.

segments.label: Pharmacokinetic Parameters
segments.value: **intermediate half-life** (~12-36 h) → less interference with the circadian rhythm compared with long-duration derivatives. **Prednisone → prednisolone** (active metabolite; hepatic first-pass essential → in patients with liver disease administer prednisolone directly). The **most versatile** among systemic GCs.

segments.label: Clinical Indications
segments.value: chronic inflammatory and autoimmune diseases, **anti-rejection therapy**, hypersensitivity/allergies; rheumatology (2nd choice after NSAIDs); locoregional **intra-articular** use (only molecules not bioactivated by the liver).

segments.label: Common Adverse Reactions (ADR)
segments.value: as for the class.

segments.label: Resistance / Efficacy Limits
segments.value: as for the class.

segments.label: Drug Combinations / Adjuvants
segments.value: + other immunosuppressants.

entries.name: Methylprednisolone
entries.memo: The **high-potency IV bolus** for immunological emergencies (MS relapse): plenty of glucocorticoid, **zero mineralocorticoid**.

segments.label: Mechanism of Action
segments.value: derivative with a double bond and a **methyl ester** → ↑ glucocorticoid vs mineralocorticoid activity.

segments.label: Molecular Target
segments.value: GR.

segments.label: Pharmacokinetic Parameters
segments.value: **intermediate duration** (like prednisolone). Widespread oral use; **succinate** ester (hydrophilic) → IV; **acetate** ester → IM as a **depot form** (long duration).

segments.label: Clinical Indications
segments.value: allergic conditions (fever/serum reactions, urticaria); **severe asthma attack** (point/resolving use, + bronchodilators — **never chronic** by the systemic route); prophylaxis of respiratory distress in **preterm birth**.

segments.label: Common Adverse Reactions (ADR)
segments.value: as for the class.

segments.label: Resistance / Efficacy Limits
segments.value: chronic systemic use contraindicated (axis suppression already after ~5 days).

segments.label: Drug Combinations / Adjuvants
segments.value: + bronchodilators (asthma).

entries.name: Triamcinolone
entries.memo: The **intra-articular** corticosteroid: the 16α-OH retains it within the joint, mineralocorticoid activity **abolished**.

segments.label: Mechanism of Action
segments.value: ↑ lipophilicity → ↑ glucocorticoid activity, **mineralocorticoid activity abolished (~0)**.

segments.label: Molecular Target
segments.value: GR.

segments.label: Pharmacokinetic Parameters
segments.value: intermediate duration.

segments.label: Clinical Indications
segments.value: allergic and inflammatory conditions.

segments.label: Common Adverse Reactions (ADR)
segments.value: as for the class.

segments.label: Resistance / Efficacy Limits
segments.value: as for the class.

segments.label: Drug Combinations / Adjuvants
segments.value: No specific data in the documents.

entries.name: Betamethasone and Dexamethasone (fluorinated)
entries.memo: The **9α-fluoro** makes them up to **30× cortisol**, long duration, zero mineralocorticoid: dexamethasone of the cytokine storm.

segments.label: Mechanism of Action
segments.value: introduction of **fluorine** → **more potent and lipophilic** glucocorticoids, very high anti-inflammatory activity, **negligible mineralocorticoid**.

segments.label: Molecular Target
segments.value: GR.

segments.label: Pharmacokinetic Parameters
segments.value: **long duration of action (up to 72 h)**; high potency (very low equivalent dose).

segments.label: Clinical Indications
segments.value: **Betamethasone** (methyl derivative of triamcinolone): critical/severe conditions **for short periods**; oral (effervescent phosphate), IV/IM (hydrophilic phosphate), hydrophobic esters (valerates/benzoates) for dermatological use; also useful for **pain** associated with post-operative oedema and in intra-articular administration. **Dexamethasone** (sodium phosphate, hydrophilic, parenteral): **cerebral oedema**.

segments.label: Common Adverse Reactions (ADR)
segments.value: profile of undesirable effects proportional to potency (as for the class).

segments.label: Resistance / Efficacy Limits
segments.value: **time-limited use** (no chronically elevated concentrations).

segments.label: Drug Combinations / Adjuvants
segments.value: as for the class.

entries.name: Fludrocortisone
entries.memo: The only glucocorticoid that works as a **pure mineralocorticoid**: it reabsorbs sodium → Addison's and orthostatic hypotension.

segments.label: Mechanism of Action
segments.value: structural variations with the opposite effect: **it loses glucocorticoid activity and gains mineralocorticoid activity**.

segments.label: Molecular Target
segments.value: MR (predominant).

segments.label: Pharmacokinetic Parameters
segments.value: No details in the documents.

segments.label: Clinical Indications
segments.value: **NOT** used as an anti-inflammatory → mineralocorticoid indication (different).

segments.label: Common Adverse Reactions (ADR)
segments.value: No specific data in the documents.

segments.label: Resistance / Efficacy Limits
segments.value: N/A in the provided documents.

segments.label: Drug Combinations / Adjuvants
segments.value: N/A in the provided documents.

entries.name: Topical and inhaled glucocorticoids (beclometasone, ciclesonide, budesonide, fluticasone; cutaneous)
entries.memo: **Total hepatic first-pass**: they act in the bronchus with minimal systemic effects. Rinse your mouth or **candida** sets in.

segments.label: Rationale
segments.value: **different molecules** from the systemic ones (the latter, if used topically, would give almost complete systemic absorption). Prodrugs bioactivated locally or molecules inactivated by hepatic first-pass are chosen → **minimal systemic exposure**.

segments.label: Inhaled/topical (prodrugs from bronchial esterases)
segments.value: **Beclometasone dipropionate** (oral bioavailability ~20%; aerosol), **Ciclesonide** (oral bioavailability <1%).

segments.label: Inactivated by hepatic first-pass
segments.value: **Budesonide** (**gastro-resistant controlled-release** formulations → IBD, intestinal topical action; also inhaled), **Fluticasone** (inhaled/topical, negligible systemic effect).

segments.label: Cutaneous by potency
segments.value: low = hydrocortisone acetate; medium = clobetasone butyrate, hydrocortisone butyrate; high = beclometasone/betamethasone dipropionate, diflucortolone, fluticasone propionate; very high = clobetasol propionate, alcinonide. **Vehicle:** ointment (lipophilic) ↑ absorption; lotion/gel ↓ potency. Dose according to **FTU (finger-tip unit)**.

segments.label: Common Adverse Reactions (ADR)
segments.value: cutaneous → **skin atrophy, folliculitis, hypertrichosis** (androgen-like action), **hypersensitivity** (rare, **non-cross-reactive**; often from excipients/preservatives), rapid tolerance. In **children** ↑ systemic absorption (surface area/weight ratio) → systemic effects (hyperglycaemia, ↓ GH, axis suppression).

segments.label: Resistance / Efficacy Limits
segments.value: systemic absorption ↑ with application surface area, altered skin barrier (burns), enhancers (iontophoresis/sonophoresis/laser), occlusive dressings.

segments.label: Drug Combinations / Adjuvants
segments.value: as for the class (for the absorbed fraction).

entries.name: NSAIDs (class — mechanism, actions, side effects)
entries.memo: They block **COX-1 and COX-2**: less pain, fever and inflammation, but also less protection → **gastric ulcer** and **renal damage**.

segments.label: Mechanism of Action
segments.value: **weak acids**; they inhibit **cyclooxygenase (COX)**. **COX-1** (constitutive) → thromboxane A2 (platelet aggregation), gastric cytoprotection (↑ mucus/bicarbonate, inhibition of the proton pump via PGE₂/EP3 receptor), renal perfusion. **COX-2** (inducible) → mediators of inflammation. Classic **non-selective** NSAIDs: steric hindrance in the catalytic site → they block the entry of arachidonic acid into **both**. **Coxibs**: they bind an accessory sulfonamide pocket **only of COX-2**.

segments.label: Molecular Target
segments.value: COX-1 and/or COX-2.

segments.label: Pharmacokinetic Parameters
segments.value: classification by **half-life**: short <6h (ASA, diclofenac, ibuprofen, indometacin, ketoprofen), medium 6-12h (ketorolac, etodolac, nimesulide), long >12h (piroxicam, naproxen, meloxicam). **Chemical** classification: propionic acid (-en), acetic acid (-ac), salicylic, fenamic, sulfonamides (coxibs), enolic (oxicams).

segments.label: Clinical Indications
segments.value: **3 actions** — **anti-inflammatory** (↓ PG from COX-2), **analgesic** (↓ nociceptor sensitization: PGs ↑ conduction via PKA → Ca/Na entry; role of **TRPV1** channels), **antipyretic** (↓ hypothalamic setpoint).

segments.label: Common Adverse Reactions (ADR)
segments.value: **GI/gastric injury** (ulcer, haemorrhage; favoured by **ion trapping** — undissociated acid crosses and accumulates in the gastric cell); **platelets** (↓ aggregation → haemorrhagic risk); **renal** (↓ prostacyclins → hypoperfusion/AKI — risk in hypertensives/CV/CKD/diuretics); **cardiovascular** (see coxibs); **CNS** (migraine, vertigo, hyperventilation — salicylates); **uterus** (indicated in primary dysmenorrhoea, **contraindicated in pregnancy** → prolongation of gestation, closure of the ductus arteriosus); **hypersensitivity** (vasomotor rhinitis, angioneurotic oedema, **asthma** — free leukotrienes, urticaria, shock).

segments.label: Resistance / Efficacy Limits
segments.value: **contraindications** — cardiovascular and renal alterations, hypersensitivity (especially to ASA), pregnancy/breastfeeding. **Paracetamol** = safer alternative (analgesic-antipyretic, not anti-inflammatory).

segments.label: Drug Combinations / Adjuvants
segments.value: **PPIs** or **misoprostol** (PG analogue, stimulates EP3) for gastroprotection; ⚠️ **+ glucocorticoids** → ↑ gastric injury; **+ anticoagulants** → ↑ haemorrhagic risk. **ASA + NSAIDs** → competition for COX-1 (see ASA card).

entries.name: Acetylsalicylic acid (ASA / Aspirin)
entries.memo: **It acetylates COX irreversibly**: the anucleate platelet cannot repair itself → 100 mg = antiplatelet for 7-10 days.

segments.label: Mechanism of Action
segments.value: **IRREVERSIBLE** inhibitor by **acetylation** (Ser of COX-1 and COX-2) → prevents the binding of arachidonic acid.

segments.label: Molecular Target
segments.value: COX-1 and COX-2 (acetylation).

segments.label: Pharmacokinetic Parameters
segments.value: absorption in the stomach and **especially the intestine**; peak 1-2h; **short half-life** (hydrolysed in the plasma/liver); renal elimination. **Dose dependence:** low doses → analgesic/antipyretic; high doses (max **4 g/day**) → anti-inflammatory.

segments.label: Clinical Indications
segments.value: inflammation, fever, rheumatic disease; **ANTIPLATELET at low doses (75-325 mg)** → secondary prevention of myocardial infarction (Patrono). *Kinetic rationale:* at low doses it acts on the **platelets in the portal circulation** (high concentration) before systemic dilution → **cumulative blockade** of platelets (lacking a nucleus → they do not resynthesize the enzyme) without systemic exposure.

segments.label: Common Adverse Reactions (ADR)
segments.value: **gastric injury** (accentuated by the irreversibility); hypersensitivity; **AKI** (elderly, CKD, children); **Reye's syndrome** in children (encephalopathy + hepatic toxicity → **contraindicated < 12 years**); **aspirin-induced asthma** (leukotrienes); salicylate poisoning (hyperventilation, **respiratory alkalosis**, suppression of the respiratory centre).

segments.label: Resistance / Efficacy Limits
segments.value: in paediatrics only **paracetamol and ibuprofen** are permitted.

segments.label: Drug Combinations / Adjuvants
segments.value: ↑ effect of **anticoagulants/antiplatelets** (synergy → haemorrhage); **+ glucocorticoids** → gastric injury; alters hypoglycaemics (hyperkalaemia → insulin); ↓ diuretics. ⚠️ **ASA + another NSAID** (e.g. ibuprofen): competition for COX-1 → the NSAID can **temporarily block the acetylation site** → ASA does not acetylate irreversibly → **loss of antiplatelet activity** → ↑ cardiovascular risk.

entries.name: Propionic acid derivatives (ibuprofen, ketoprofen, naproxen, flurbiprofen, fenoprofen)
entries.memo: **Reversible** inhibition of COX-1/2 and short half-life: the most manageable and widely used NSAID.

segments.label: Mechanism of Action
segments.value: **REVERSIBLE** inhibitors of COX; generally **non-selective** (some more COX-1: flurbiprofen, ketoprofen).

segments.label: Molecular Target
segments.value: COX-1/COX-2.

segments.label: Pharmacokinetic Parameters
segments.value: well absorbed orally, **almost complete binding to albumin**. **Ibuprofen**: 200-800 mg every 4-6 h, the **best tolerated** (least gastric injury).

segments.label: Clinical Indications
segments.value: common inflammations; chronic treatment of **rheumatoid arthritis and osteoarthritis**.

segments.label: Common Adverse Reactions (ADR)
segments.value: **better GI tolerability** (vs other NSAIDs).

segments.label: Resistance / Efficacy Limits
segments.value: as for the class.

segments.label: Drug Combinations / Adjuvants
segments.value: see ASA + NSAIDs.

entries.name: Diclofenac (acetic acid derivative)
entries.memo: **COX-2 preferential**: an excellent anti-inflammatory but the most **cardiotoxic** among the commonly used NSAIDs.

segments.label: Mechanism of Action
segments.value: **greater selectivity for COX-2** → greater anti-inflammatory activity, fewer side effects (it spares COX-1).

segments.label: Molecular Target
segments.value: COX-2 (preferential).

segments.label: Pharmacokinetic Parameters
segments.value: rapid absorption, **high protein binding**, half-life **1-2 h**; hepatic metabolism via **CYP2C** → glucuronidation/sulfation → urinary/biliary excretion.

segments.label: Clinical Indications
segments.value: RA, osteoarthritis, pain, primary dysmenorrhoea (100-200 mg).

segments.label: Common Adverse Reactions (ADR)
segments.value: reduced (COX-2 selectivity).

segments.label: Resistance / Efficacy Limits
segments.value: No specific data.

segments.label: Drug Combinations / Adjuvants
segments.value: **peculiarity**: at low pH (inflammation) it **accumulates in inflamed joints/tissues**; possible inhibition of the **NF-κB** cascade (additional anti-inflammatory potential).

entries.name: Indometacin (acetic acid derivative)
entries.memo: Potent and toxic; the blockade of PGE2 is exploited to **close the ductus arteriosus** in the preterm infant.

segments.label: Mechanism of Action
segments.value: **non-selective** NSAID, fairly **potent**.

segments.label: Molecular Target
segments.value: COX-1/COX-2.

segments.label: Pharmacokinetic Parameters
segments.value: peak 1-2h; **enterohepatic recirculation** (glucuronidated metabolites cleaved by the intestinal flora → reabsorption of the base form).

segments.label: Clinical Indications
segments.value: chronic diseases (osteoarthritis, rheumatoid arthritis).

segments.label: Common Adverse Reactions (ADR)
segments.value: **significant nephrotoxicity** → not in common use (an option when the better-tolerated NSAIDs do not respond); enterohepatic recirculation prolongs the undesirable effects.

segments.label: Resistance / Efficacy Limits
segments.value: use limited by toxicity.

segments.label: Drug Combinations / Adjuvants
segments.value: No specific data in the documents.

entries.name: Ketorolac (Toradol — acetic acid derivative)
entries.memo: NSAID analgesia **comparable to opioids**, but **maximum 5 days**: beyond that, the kidney gives way.

segments.label: Mechanism of Action
segments.value: NSAID with **strong analgesic potential**; more selective for **COX-1**.

segments.label: Molecular Target
segments.value: COX-1 (preferential).

segments.label: Pharmacokinetic Parameters
segments.value: medium half-life (6-12h).

segments.label: Clinical Indications
segments.value: analgesic power **comparable to opioids** → **moderate-severe post-operative pain**, alternative to weak opioids; **only short-term (max 5 days)**.

segments.label: Common Adverse Reactions (ADR)
segments.value: **strong gastric injury** (proportional to the analgesic power).

segments.label: Resistance / Efficacy Limits
segments.value: use limited to the short term.

segments.label: Drug Combinations / Adjuvants
segments.value: advantage vs opioids → **no respiratory depression, no dependence** (at the cost of gastric injury).

entries.name: Nimesulide (Aulin)
entries.memo: COX-2 preferential (stomach protected) but **hepatotoxic**: maximum 15 days.

segments.label: Mechanism of Action
segments.value: NSAID (medium half-life).

segments.label: Molecular Target
segments.value: COX (COX-2 oriented).

segments.label: Pharmacokinetic Parameters
segments.value: medium half-life (6-12h).

segments.label: Clinical Indications
segments.value: restricted by AIFA to **acute pain** and **primary dysmenorrhoea** (NOT chronic pain); **2nd line**, **maximum 15 days**.

segments.label: Common Adverse Reactions (ADR)
segments.value: **hepatotoxicity** (EMA/AIFA reports) → little prescribed today.

segments.label: Resistance / Efficacy Limits
segments.value: restricted indications because of the hepatic/GI risk.

segments.label: Drug Combinations / Adjuvants
segments.value: No specific data in the documents.

entries.name: Oxicams (piroxicam, meloxicam)
entries.memo: **Long half-life (24h)**, once daily owing to the enterohepatic circulation; the price: marked **gastric injury**.

segments.label: Mechanism of Action
segments.value: NSAIDs derived from enolic acids; **non-selective** for COX-2 but "**oriented**".

segments.label: Molecular Target
segments.value: COX (COX-2 oriented).

segments.label: Pharmacokinetic Parameters
segments.value: **long half-life** (>12h) → **single daily administration**.

segments.label: Clinical Indications
segments.value: **chronic** conditions (arthritis, osteoarthritis, rheumatoid arthritis).

segments.label: Common Adverse Reactions (ADR)
segments.value: as for the class.

segments.label: Resistance / Efficacy Limits
segments.value: No specific data.

segments.label: Drug Combinations / Adjuvants
segments.value: No specific data in the documents.

entries.name: Coxibs (celecoxib, valdecoxib, rofecoxib)
entries.memo: **COX-2 selective**: they save the stomach but tip the balance towards **thrombosis** (rofecoxib was withdrawn because of myocardial infarctions).

segments.label: Mechanism of Action
segments.value: **COX-2 selective** (accessory sulfonamide pocket).

segments.label: Molecular Target
segments.value: COX-2 (selective).

segments.label: Pharmacokinetic Parameters
segments.value: No details in the documents.

segments.label: Clinical Indications
segments.value: analgesic, anti-inflammatory, antipyretic with **low potential for gastric injury** and platelet alteration (TXA2 unaffected). **Celecoxib** → abdominal pain.

segments.label: Common Adverse Reactions (ADR)
segments.value: ⚠️ **increased cardiovascular risk** (↓ vasodilating prostacyclins against active COX-1/TXA2 → prothrombotic imbalance); **first coxibs withdrawn from the market** because of serious CV events.

segments.label: Resistance / Efficacy Limits
segments.value: **contraindicated** in patients at cardiovascular risk.

segments.label: Drug Combinations / Adjuvants
segments.value: No specific data in the documents.

entries.name: Paracetamol (atypical NSAID)
entries.memo: Safe up to a threshold, then **NAPQI** depletes glutathione → hepatic necrosis. Antidote: **N-acetylcysteine**.

segments.label: Mechanism of Action
segments.value: different chemical structure; **poor inhibitor of COX-1/COX-2**. Activity on a **"central COX-3"** (antipyretic effect). Analgesia not fully clarified → **central** action (↓ central PGs), **descending inhibitory pathways** (serotonergic/noradrenergic), **endogenous opioid peptides**, **endocannabinoid system** (metabolite conjugated with arachidonic acid → **AM404**, anandamide-like → CB1 + desensitized TRPV). **Analgesic and antipyretic only, NOT anti-inflammatory.**

segments.label: Molecular Target
segments.value: central COX-3; serotonergic/noradrenergic/opioid/endocannabinoid systems.

segments.label: Pharmacokinetic Parameters
segments.value: **excellent** oral **bioavailability**; peak 30-60 min; **short half-life (2h)**; hepatic metabolism; renal elimination. **No gastric injury or platelet effect.**

segments.label: Clinical Indications
segments.value: mild-moderate pain and fever; **one of the few drugs usable in paediatrics** (NSAIDs carry renal risk in children); dose sparing on opioids (↓ tolerance). Dose: 1000 mg/day inhibits ~50% COX-1/2.

segments.label: Common Adverse Reactions (ADR)
segments.value: ⚠️ **HEPATIC DAMAGE** — the metabolite **N-acetyl-benzoquinone-imine** (via **CYP2E1**) is normally detoxified by **glutathione**; if glutathione is depleted (excessive doses / wrong frequency) → **hepatotoxicity**. (<4 g low risk; >10 g severe insult.)

segments.label: Resistance / Efficacy Limits
segments.value: safe in children, pregnancy, platelet problems, CV events, hypertension, nephropathies (liver disease with caution — the liver is the "Achilles' heel").

segments.label: Drug Combinations / Adjuvants
segments.value: **antidote for poisoning = N-acetylcysteine** (thiol groups, replaces glutathione); ⚠️ **ethanol** induces CYP2E1 → ↑ hepatotoxic risk.

entries.name: Histamine — receptors and pharmacological strategies
entries.memo: Four receptors, four worlds: **H1** allergy/CNS, **H2** gastric acid, H3 brake, H4 immunity.

segments.label: Receptors (4 isotypes, all GPCRs; drugs only for H1 and H2)
segments.value:
• **H1** (Gq → PLC → DAG/IP3 → ↑ Ca²⁺): endothelium (NO → **vasodilation**), bronchial/intestinal smooth muscle (**bronchoconstriction**, ↑ peristalsis/cramps), CTZ/**vestibular apparatus** (nausea/vomiting), **hypothalamus** (feeding behaviour, **wakefulness**), skin (wheal, flushing, ↑ permeability), nerve endings (**itch/pain**).
• **H2** (Gs → AC → ↑ cAMP): **gastric parietal cell** (↑ proton pump → HCl), heart (↑ chronotropism/inotropism).
• **H3** (CNS, inhibitory control of NT release) and **H4** (immune cells): no drug available.

segments.label: Pharmacological strategies
segments.value: (1) **H1/H2 antagonists** (= **inverse agonists** → they stabilize the resting form of the constitutively active receptor); (2) **inhibitors of mast cell degranulation** (**sodium cromoglicate** → useful in **prophylaxis**, ineffective against already-released histamine); (3) **functional antagonism** (**adrenaline** in anaphylactic shock: α1 ↑ blood pressure, β1 stimulates the heart, bronchodilation — faster than antihistamines).

entries.name: 1st-generation anti-H1 (chlorphenamine, diphenhydramine, hydroxyzine, promethazine)
entries.memo: They cross the blood-brain barrier → **sedation** + antimuscarinic effects: they are the over-the-counter sleep aids.

segments.label: Mechanism of Action
segments.value: inverse agonists of the **H1** receptor (they do not act on H2); **high lipophilicity** → they cross the **blood-brain barrier**.

segments.label: Molecular Target
segments.value: H1 receptor (+ **poor selectivity** → also muscarinic, α-adrenergic, serotonergic receptors).

segments.label: Pharmacokinetic Parameters
segments.value: excellent; high lipophilicity, high Vd, uniform distribution; hepatic metabolism; urinary elimination.

segments.label: Clinical Indications
segments.value: symptomatic treatment of **allergic reactions** (rhinitis, urticaria, dermatitis); **antiemetics** (motion sickness/vestibular origin); **sedative-hypnotics** (promethazine); **Parkinson's** (anticholinergic activity to counterbalance the dopaminergic deficit). Little used as antiallergics because of the sedation.

segments.label: Common Adverse Reactions (ADR)
segments.value: **CNS** (sedation, ↓ alertness, ↓ psychomotor activity, **↑ appetite/weight** — hypothalamic H1 + serotonergic antagonism); **antimuscarinic** (dry mouth, urinary retention, **sinus tachycardia** from M2 antagonism); **anti-α-adrenergic** (orthostatic hypotension, vertigo, reflex tachycardia); **teratogenic** (discontinue in pregnancy). Overdose: hallucinations, excitation, coma, ataxia, convulsions.

segments.label: Resistance / Efficacy Limits
segments.value: **unfavourable** risk-benefit ratio (poor receptor selectivity).

segments.label: Drug Combinations / Adjuvants
segments.value: ⚠️ they potentiate other CNS depressants (**benzodiazepines**, sedative-hypnotics, **alcohol**); **antipsychotics** (summation of anticholinergic/anti-α1/antihistamine effects); **MAOIs** (potentiate the anticholinergic effect).

entries.name: 2nd-generation anti-H1 (loratadine, cetirizine) — *withdrawn: terfenadine, astemizole*
entries.memo: **They do not cross the BBB** → antihistamines without drowsiness (cetirizine remains the most sedating of the group).

segments.label: Mechanism of Action
segments.value: they block H1 with **less crossing of the BBB** → **peripheral effects only**; **better selectivity** (no antimuscarinic/anti-α).

segments.label: Molecular Target
segments.value: H1 receptor (peripheral).

segments.label: Pharmacokinetic Parameters
segments.value: **piperidines** (loratadine), **piperazines** (cetirizine); greater affinity/potency; lower lipophilicity; well absorbed orally; **CYP450** metabolism; urinary excretion; **longer duration of action**.

segments.label: Clinical Indications
segments.value: **allergic reactions only** (no sedation, no vestibular/appetite effects).

segments.label: Common Adverse Reactions (ADR)
segments.value: ⚠️ **Terfenadine/Astemizole** (withdrawn) → **torsades de pointes, QT prolongation** (interaction with K⁺ channels) + cytochrome inhibition. Loratadine/cetirizine → cardiac concern with prolonged use (monitor).

segments.label: Resistance / Efficacy Limits
segments.value: cardiac monitoring in chronic therapy.

segments.label: Drug Combinations / Adjuvants
segments.value: ⚠️ with **CYP inhibitors** → ↑ plasma concentrations → cardiac effects.

entries.name: 3rd-generation anti-H1 (desloratadine, levocetirizine, fexofenadine; topical: azelastine, levocabastine, ketotifen)
entries.memo: **Active metabolites ready to go**: they bypass the cytochrome → fewer interactions.

segments.label: Mechanism of Action
segments.value: **purified active stereoisomers** of the 2nd generation (**desloratadine** = dextrorotatory form of loratadine; **levocetirizine** = levorotatory form of cetirizine) → lower doses, ↓ side effects. **Fexofenadine** = prodrug with active metabolites.

segments.label: Molecular Target
segments.value: H1 receptor (peripheral).

segments.label: Pharmacokinetic Parameters
segments.value: reduced doses; **they do not cross the BBB** → no sedation → can be administered in the **morning**.

segments.label: Clinical Indications
segments.value: allergic reactions safely (even during working activity). **Topical** (azelastine, levocabastine, ketotifen): dermatological preparations/urticaria, prophylaxis of the asthma attack. **Ketotifen**: stabilization of the mast cell membrane (similar to the cromones).

segments.label: Common Adverse Reactions (ADR)
segments.value: **free of cardiotoxicity** (reduced doses).

segments.label: Drug Combinations / Adjuvants
segments.value: as for the 2nd generation, but with a better safety profile.

entries.name: Anti-H2 (cimetidine, ranitidine, famotidine, nizatidine, roxatidine)
entries.memo: They block the **H2 of the parietal cell** → less HCl; today second choice after the PPIs.

segments.label: Mechanism of Action
segments.value: **selective competitive antagonists of the H2 receptor** on the parietal cell → ↓ **PKA-mediated** stimulation of the proton pump → ↓ acid secretion (**basal, nocturnal, food-stimulated**; to a lesser extent that induced by gastrin and ACh). It is a **modulation, not a definitive blockade** → **it does not compromise vitamin B12 absorption**.

segments.label: Molecular Target
segments.value: gastric H2 receptor.

segments.label: Pharmacokinetic Parameters
segments.value: structure similar to histamine (no lipophilic component → **no BBB → no central effects**); important **hepatic first-pass** (cimetidine, ranitidine, famotidine); **renal tubular** excretion by active secretion (caution in CKD); **nizatidine** (fluorine atom) → better bioavailability. Half-life not long, effect ~6-8 h.

segments.label: Clinical Indications
segments.value: **Zollinger-Ellison syndrome**, gastric/duodenal ulcer, *H. pylori* ulcer (multiple therapy), gastroesophageal reflux, stress ulcer.

segments.label: Common Adverse Reactions (ADR)
segments.value: poor antimuscarinic/antidopaminergic/antiserotonergic/anti-α/antiallergic activity. Non-specific: headache, vertigo, nausea, erythema, itch, haematological effects (↓ bone marrow activity). ⚠️ **Cimetidine**: it binds the androgen receptor, interferes with estradiol/prolactin → **loss of libido, impotence, gynaecomastia** (in men), alterations of the menstrual cycle.

segments.label: Resistance / Efficacy Limits
segments.value: **cimetidine** (interactions) and **ranitidine** (contamination with carcinogenic **nitrosamines**) **no longer on the market**.

segments.label: Drug Combinations / Adjuvants
segments.value: ⚠️ **Cimetidine = potent CYP450 inhibitor** → ↑ concentrations of barbiturates, benzodiazepines, antidepressants, antipsychotics, calcium channel blockers, sulfonylureas (the other molecules do not share this inhibition).
